mnemonics: Uniformly Continuous is a Uniform Rule: The δ (delta) you choose depends only on ε (epsilon), not on the point x. Continuous can be a Custom Rule: The δ can depend on both ε and the specific point x.
keyPoints: A function is continuous if, for every point and every ε > 0, there exists a δ > 0 (which can depend on the point and ε) such that points within δ map to within ε.
A function is uniformly continuous if, for every ε > 0, there exists a δ > 0 (depending only on ε) that works for ALL points in the domain simultaneously.
Every uniformly continuous function is continuous, but the converse is not true (e.g., f(x)=1/x on (0,1) is continuous but not uniformly continuous).
commonMistakes: Assuming that if a function is continuous at every point, it must be uniformly continuous. This is only true on compact sets.
Trying to prove a function is uniformly continuous by choosing a δ that depends on a specific point, rather than finding a single δ that works globally for a given ε.
